text.title: Idempotency, Leader Election, Rate Limiting, and Pattern Combinations
text.summary: Idempotency implementation strategies, HTTP method idempotency, Leader Election algorithms, Rate Limiting algorithm selection, and common pattern combinations for production scenarios.

rule.statement: Store the idempotency record and its response after the transaction commits, not before, using the idempotency key as the primary key.
rule.rationale: Storing before the transaction means a commit failure leaves a stale "already processed" record with no result — subsequent retries are silently dropped. Storing after ensures the record exists only when the operation actually succeeded.

list.item: Idempotency Key: client-generated UUID per operation, stored in cache/DB with TTL. Return same response for same key.
Natural Key: business identifier (account_id + amount + reference_id). Hash to create deterministic key. No client cooperation required.
ETag / Version: optimistic concurrency control. Client includes If-Match header; server rejects if version mismatch.
Message Deduplication: queue-level dedup window (SQS: 5min; Kafka: enable.idempotence = true).

rule.statement: Set an expiration time on idempotency keys (typically 24h-7d) to prevent indefinite storage growth.
rule.rationale: Without expiration, idempotency records accumulate indefinitely. After the safe retry window (client's retry policy maximum), old keys are no longer needed.

list.item: etcd / Consul: consensus-based (Raft algorithm), production-grade, use for distributed coordination (Kubernetes, Kafka KRaft).
Redis SETNX + TTL (Redlock): simpler, sufficient for single-datacenter; not safe across multiple independent Redis instances.
Database row lock + heartbeat: suitable when a database is already available; leader holds a lock row and renews on interval.

rule.statement: Always set a TTL on leader locks; the leader must renew the lock before expiry, not after re-election.
rule.rationale: A leader that pauses (GC, network partition) without TTL holds the lock indefinitely, blocking all other candidates. TTL ensures automatic failover within a bounded time window.

list.item: Token Bucket: allows controlled bursts up to bucket size, smooth average rate. Best for API rate limiting where occasional bursts are acceptable.
Leaky Bucket: fixed output rate regardless of input burst. Best for traffic shaping to downstream with fixed capacity.
Fixed Window: simple, but allows 2x rate at window boundaries. Use only for rough limiting.
Sliding Window: accurate, no boundary spikes. Best for precise per-client rate control.
E-commerce order: Saga + Outbox + Idempotency Key
API resilience: Circuit Breaker + Bulkhead + Retry with jitter
Event streaming: Event Sourcing + CQRS + Outbox
